vocab.translation: to take, grab, seize (Spain: standard; Latin America: vulgar slang—avoid)
vocab.note: In Spain, 'coger' is neutral and common. In most of Latin America, it has offensive connotations. Better alternatives: 'tomar', 'agarrar', 'tomar prestado' (to borrow).

vocab.translation: to take, to grab (but has vulgar sexual meaning in Latin America)
vocab.note: In Mexico, Argentina, and other Latin American countries, 'coger' has a crude sexual meaning. Safer alternatives are 'tomar' or 'agarrar'.
Preferred form in Latin America instead of 'coger el autobús'.
Safe alternative to 'coger' in all Spanish-speaking regions.

vocab.translation: to take / to grab (Spain); vulgar/sexual meaning in Latin America
vocab.note: In Spain: normal usage (coger el metro = take the metro). In Latin America: offensive/vulgar. Use 'tomar' or 'agarrar' instead to avoid confusion.
Neutral alternative to 'coger' in Latin America (tomar el metro = take the metro).
Neutral alternative to 'coger' in Latin America.
Common phrase to check understanding
Alternative way to verify understanding

vocab.translation: Do I explain myself? / Am I making myself clear?
vocab.note: Alternative way to ask if someone understands

mistakes.description: Dijo "practicar un poquito de español para ahora"
mistakes.correction: Lo correcto es "por ahora" o simplemente "ahora". "Por" expresa duración/momento temporal; "para" indica destino o propósito.

mistakes.description: Uso excesivo del pronombre "yo" en cada oración ("yo hice", "yo trabajo", "yo tengo", "yo quiero")
mistakes.correction: En español el pronombre sujeto suele omitirse porque la conjugación del verbo ya indica la persona. Solo se usa "yo" para enfatizar o contrastar.

"Especializarse" es reflexivo: "quiero especializarme en...". Y "sistema" es masculino (el sistema, los sistemas) a pesar de terminar en -a, porque viene del griego, como "problema", "tema", "programa".

mistakes.description: Learner said "Yo voy a empezar con..." when the pronoun was unnecessary.
mistakes.correction: In Spanish, subject pronouns (yo, tú, etc.) are usually omitted because the verb ending already indicates who is performing the action. Say "voy a empezar con..." instead of "yo voy a empezar con..."

mistakes.description: Learner used "donde vivo" when the tutor had asked "donde vives" — failing to adjust the verb form to match the question's perspective (tú vs. yo).
mistakes.correction: When answering a question asked in tú form, switch the verb conjugation to match your response perspective. If asked "¿dónde vives?" answer "vivo"; if asked "¿qué haces?" answer "hago" — not repeating the tú conjugation from the question.

vocab.note: Demonstrates genitive after из

preferences: Translate most Russian phrases into English in parentheses, since the learner is a beginner in Russian.

vocab.note: Also מה שלומך (shlomech for feminine singular). Formal way to ask how someone is.
Common greeting question in Swedish
Used to express how you feel: Jag mår bra = I'm doing well.
From 'hur mår du?' (how are you?); 'jag mår bra' (I'm well)
Also used as 'tack för att du frågar' (thanks for asking)
bor = live/reside
i New York fortfarande = still in New York
någon = some/any; annanstans = elsewhere
Common question asking about someone's residence
Used to state where you live, followed by a place name
First person singular present tense of 'arbeta'
First person singular present tense of 'studera'
Common question phrase
Alternative to 'arbetar'; more colloquial
Used in 'jobba inom teknologi' – within/in the field of technology
Used in 'jobba med teknik' – work with technology
att skriva kod (writing code)

vocab.translation: What programming language do you use?
vocab.note: Question structure useful for language discussion
Standard way to express what you use in Swedish
Alternative phrasing specific to programming languages

mistakes.description: Learner said "För du i dag" instead of a complete phrase like "Hur mår du?" (How are you?)
mistakes.correction: "Hur mår du?" (How are you?) — "För" means "for" and doesn't work as an opening greeting. The correct phrase for asking how someone is uses "Hur mår du?" (literally: How feels you?)

mistakes.description: Learner said "Jag är god" (I am good) for expressing how they feel.
mistakes.correction: "Jag mår bra" is the correct way to say "I'm doing well" or "I feel good." "Mår" (from "må") is used for how you feel; "god" (good) is mainly for food, things, or describing someone as kind.
